label: analysis

text: NewJeans siding with Min Heejin makes complete sense — they were in an ADOR bubble, she showered them with gifts, built relationships with their parents, and guided them through hit after hit, so of course young girls in that orbit couldn't see it objectively.
label: analysis

text: Star quality is a myth that obscures what it takes to succeed — it's actually a varying mix of looks, marketing, timing, luck and skill, and it's not the same for everyone.
label: analysis

text: I don't care about breaking records, I just want the groups I listen to be able to make a living.
label: fandom

text: Companies are operating from fear rather than prioritizing concept artistry or musical direction — when any small thing can dismantle a career like with AOA's Jimin or Garam, entertainment companies lean toward caution in every single aspect.
label: analysis

text: Kpop used to be fun, different, and creative which drew me to it in the first place — it's mainly our fault as fans and netizens for why that no longer exists, we only have ourselves to blame.
label: reaction

text: Official group positions are slowly disappearing — everyone's an all-rounder nowadays even though some aren't really capable of being talented at everything, and it's okay to stand out in a particular skill.
label: analysis

text: No one has their own individual sound anymore — there's nothing wrong with chasing trends but when you're chasing every trend it becomes a problem.
label: analysis

text: Having a bias is a teenage thing — when everyone you interact with has a bias you subconsciously think you need one too, but once you grow out of needing to fit in certain things stop feeling compulsory.
label: analysis

text: I've been into kpop since 2010 and now I mainly just check out the music — no interviews, no performances, no variety shows, and if I have a favorite in a group it's probably because 90% of the group is nameless to me.
label: fandom

text: I now just listen to debuts and comebacks without strings attached and I'm happiest this way — I don't have to deal with the fandom side and I'm here purely for the music.
label: fandom

text: Before getting into kpop I had no connection to pop music for 8 years — in that period only artists like Adele, Taylor Swift, and Drake reached me, and by 2018 I had also come to know BTS existed despite knowing nothing about them, which made me curious enough to become a fan.
label: fandom

text: The photocard system is the worst — it makes people buy tons of albums just to get one card, and the resellers and trading market create the overconsumption and inflation problem, not necessarily the fans or companies.
label: analysis

text: The gamification of music in the kpop industry trains stans to care less about good music and more about seeking the dopamine rush from unlocking new achievements — more releases means more possible achievements.
label: analysis

text: Idols are all-around entertainers not just singers — the music releases are part of content alongside concerts, variety shows, social media, and lives, so comparing release frequency to western artists misses the point.
label: analysis

text: K-pop fandoms basically industrialized social media engagement — streaming goals, voting apps, trending hashtags — so it looks dominant online, but the actual consumer base is still much smaller than mainstream Western pop.
label: analysis

text: Kpop is like vtubing in streaming — not a hard music category but a way of presentation, and based on streaming data, hip-hop, American pop, country, and Latin all absolutely dwarf kpop streams worldwide.
label: analysis

text: Asian Pop tours have seen a roughly 600% increase between 2015 and 2024 — the bigger artists are filling arenas the same venues mainstream artists fill, yet they aren't widely known to the general public.
label: analysis

text: Kpop is not a musical genre in the sense of pop, rock, or country — it's an entertainment category with its own ecosystem and rules, and calling it a genre has zero musical substance behind it.
label: analysis

text: Lee Soo Man was directly inspired by the Japanese idol industry and Barry Gordy's Motown Records — Japan, the US, and even Detroit had all imprinted themselves on Korea by the time kpop started.
label: analysis

text: A song like Guerilla by ATEEZ isn't pop and sounds nothing like Magnetic by ILLIT, yet both are classified under kpop — which proves kpop isn't a genre but an industry defined by the idol training system and parasocial market.
label: analysis

text: BTS sold 34 copies of their first album on the first day from a bankrupt no-name company with a flooded basement practice room — Yoongi was doing side hustles because their future was shaky at best, and now they've built HYBE.
label: analysis

text: Brave Girls' Rolling is the greatest underdog story in kpop — a 4 year old song from an unknown group hit the top of charts without any promotion, right when the members were literally exploring other lines of work.
label: reaction

text: ATEEZ sold 430 copies on their first day and never held back great songs because there was a possibility they'd disband — now they're one of the only non-big 4 groups consistently on the highest concert and album sales lists.
label: reaction

text: I-dle's success was surprising twice — first with Latata rising on charts despite Cube's track record, and then again with Tomboy after a long hiatus when everyone expected it to be the end of the group.
label: analysis

text: The ETA controversy with NewJeans had almost 1k comments about terrorism before the MV came out — then complete silence when it was just a pool party shot on iPhone about a breakup, it's almost like mass psychosis looking back.
label: reaction

text: The Tablo Stanford situation is the most absurd controversy in kpop — getting an undergrad degree and masters from Stanford somehow nearly ruined his and his whole group's career through a coordinated harassment campaign.
label: reaction

text: Dawn and Hyuna getting fired by Cube for publicly dating is one of the stupidest overblown controversies — and the worst part was international fans defending Cube over it at the time.
label: reaction

text: ILLIT's Lucky Girl Syndrome is unique because the word syndrome is usually used to define something negative or abnormal, but in this song it becomes a positive and empowering trait instead.
label: analysis

text: Wings was BTS's magnum opus — the story behind it borrowed from classical literature in a way I hadn't seen in kpop before, and Stigma, Lie, and First Love are some of the best solos to come out of kpop.
label: analysis

text: Blood Sweat & Tears is underrated in BTS discussions — everything about it is absolutely perfect and I believe it changed the kpop scene tremendously at the time.
label: analysis

text: The HYYH era was the most impactful for BTS — it changed the trajectory of their careers and of kpop in general by perfectly introducing multi-media story narratives incorporating music, videos, films, books, and audience participation.
label: analysis

text: Arirang is an absolutely genius piece of work with no skips — musically and lyrically so much deeper than people gave it credit for when it first came out, and the only BTS album I must listen to from top to bottom.
label: fandom

text: All IVE members sing really well and their raw vocals in encores never disappoint — but Yujin tries her hardest to sing everything live in performances and deserves credit for that.
label: analysis

text: Leeseo deserves more appreciation for her improvement — she improved her stage presence, rap, and vocals so much in 2023 and somehow doesn't get the same attention as Rei, Liz, and Gaeul for their talent.
label: fandom

text: Wonyoung haters pmo to an extreme level — she gets so much unnecessary hate and I love her so much.
label: fandom

text: Starship needs to promote their bside tracks the way Itzy's That's a No No and Baemon's Really Like You were promoted — IVE has too many underrated bsides that deserve more attention.
label: analysis

text: Idols thrive in a group setting because member interactions and chemistry serves as a magnification of each other's charms — soloist Somi is quite different from IOI's Somi, and you need another beagle member to match her energy and get the tiki taka effect.
label: analysis

text: The IOI comeback was remarkable and by the end everyone was so comfortable opening up and showing their personalities on stage — I'm actually kinda depressed this comeback is over.
label: fandom

text: Somi wrote and produced Suddenly for the IOI comeback and even funded it partly with her own money taking a loss — she became the de facto leader once the comeback got rolling despite Chungha being the main catalyst.
label: reaction

text: The IOI group chat has been going strong since they disbanded with Seojeong messaging the most — it's bittersweet how much Chungha missed the girls after establishing herself as one of the top soloists, like she misses the simpler days surrounded by silly friends.
label: fandom

text: I.O.I. was only around for less than a year and disbanded almost eight years ago but god I still miss them — their lineup had it all, talent, visuals, personality, and I truly believe if they were a permanent group they would have been one of the greats.
label: fandom

text: They barely even had a chance to promote with all members present yet were still such an unbelievable group — it pains me to see them slowly be forgotten when they could have been so much more with the same time and quality songs later Produce groups had.
label: reaction

text: I fell in love with I.O.I's chemistry after watching one piece of content and binge-watched the entire survival show — their bond and talent was undeniable, and it's amazing that even though they were a group for such a short period they all still treasure that experience so much today.
label: fandom
